meta.description: Personal cheat sheet for which model to use with Cline for different coding tasks, based on current OpenRouter prices.
meta.author: Your Name
meta.notes: Models assume Anthropic 4.6 series, OpenAI GPT-5.3 Codex / GPT-5.4, Google Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite/Pro, KAT-Coder-Pro V1 (free tier) and Trinity-Large-Preview (free).

examples: Scaffold a brand new service or application
Initial build of a new feature that creates multiple files
Setting up routing, basic data models and initial API endpoints
usage_rules.why: Sonnet 4.6 is strong enough for architecture+implementation at lower cost than Opus; Codex is a solid alternative with long context.
usage_rules.notes: Reserve Opus 4.6 for especially complex or high-risk greenfield builds.

examples: Ask how a specific library function works
Explain an error message
Generate a small code snippet or helper function
usage_rules.why: Flash Lite is extremely cheap and good for Q&A; KAT free tier is fine when latency is acceptable.

examples: Generate boilerplate for a basic component or class
Rename functions or variables within one file
Add simple logging statements
usage_rules.why: Use free coder/general models for low-risk work to avoid API cost entirely.

examples: Fix a bug with a clear stack trace in one file
Resolve a type error or linter issue in a small module
usage_rules.why: Start on the free coder; upgrade to cheap Flash Lite when reasoning needs a bit more reliability.

examples: Create unit tests for a single module
Add a few test cases around a new function
usage_rules.why: Flash Lite is cheap and adequate; Codex if you need stronger coding intuition.

examples: Refactor a function into smaller pieces
Improve readability and structure of one file
usage_rules.why: Codex gives strong coding performance at reasonable cost; Sonnet if Codex struggles.

examples: Refactor a module and its tests
Change an interface used in a few files
usage_rules.why: Good cross-file reasoning and long context without Opus-level price.

examples: Add a new feature touching several modules or services
Introduce a new domain concept that requires multiple files
usage_rules.why: Sonnet 4.6 is tuned for iterative development and agents; upgrade to Opus only if the plan clearly needs it.

examples: Design a new system or service architecture
Review security or performance implications of a design
Evaluate alternative architecture options
usage_rules.why: Both are frontier reasoning models; Sonnet is slightly more cost-effective for long text reasoning, GPT-5.4 when multimodal or huge context matters.

examples: Understand this area of the repo and propose a plan
Analyse multiple files before a big refactor
Investigate a complex, multi-module bug
usage_rules.why: Codex has very long context at lower blended cost; Opus is reserved for the toughest, longest-running sessions.

examples: Create or refactor CI/CD pipelines
Edit Dockerfiles, compose files or deployment manifests
Tidy up complex build scripts
usage_rules.why: You want a very reliable coder here but Opus is overkill most of the time.

examples: Write README or basic project documentation
Generate migration guides or internal notes
usage_rules.why: Docs are great candidates for cheap/free models; pay more only when highly polished output is critical.

examples: Generate a regex
Write a tiny utility script
Perform simple text transformations or translations
usage_rules.why: These are perfect for the cheapest or free models.

examples: Work on code that must not leave the local machine
Experiment with proprietary algorithms or IP-sensitive logic
usage_rules.why: Use the most private / controlled setup available and avoid expensive frontier APIs.

examples: Try out ideas and toy prototypes
Play with new APIs or frameworks in small sandboxes
usage_rules.why: Exploratory work should be essentially free unless you discover a real project.
